context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on the SWOT thing for my city case study rn. idk if i’m supposed to do one big SWOT or separate ones for each pathway.
exchanges.tutor:
Look closely at this line: “Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway.” What does that suggest about the structure—one city-wide SWOT, or a SWOT organized pathway by pathway?

A useful next step: name the 6 pathways you’re using, then decide whether each needs its own row/section with strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats. If you want, send me how your table is laid out and I’ll help you check whether it matches the assignment.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will clarify the assignment structure without doing the work for the student. To stay within the tutor role and use guided discovery, I will point the student back to the assignment language, then ask a narrowing question that helps them infer the required format. I will keep it brief and avoid giving content for the SWOT itself.

exchanges.student: ok so maybe it’s separate by pathway, but that still feels super broad tbh. can u just explain the whole assignment in simple words bc i’m still not getting what they want.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—simple version:

You’re pulling together your earlier work on your city and judging it pathway by pathway using SWOT.

What they want you to do:
1. Look back at your Spider Diagram: which pathways are weakest?
2. Look back at your Power/Actor Map: who helps or blocks action?
3. Look back at your stressors: what climate pressures matter most?
4. For each pathway, list internal positives/negatives and external positives/risks.
5. Then pick the 2 best opportunities for action across the whole analysis.
6. Use that to discuss 1 policy recommendation in the forum.

So this is less “write an essay” and more “organize your evidence into the table.”

What’s your city, and which pathway feels most confusing right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will simplify the assignment into its core tasks while preserving the student's responsibility to interpret and complete it. I will not provide a filled example or wording for their submission. I will use concise guided breakdown and end with a question that moves them toward action on their own city and materials.

exchanges.student: it’s still all blending together ngl, like spider diagram vs actor map vs stressors all sound like the same info to me. that didn’t help much, can u give me a more complete example of how one pathway would look?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re blending different kinds of evidence. Separate them by question:

Spider Diagram = How strong or weak is each pathway overall?
Actor Map = Who has power to move or block action?
Stressors = What climate pressures/problems is the city dealing with?
SWOT = Given all that, what helps, hurts, could be leveraged, or could derail progress in this pathway?

Try one pathway with just these 4 prompts:
1. Is this pathway currently strong or weak?
2. Which actors matter most here?
3. Which stressors make this pathway urgent?
4. From those, what belongs under S, W, O, and T?

Pick one pathway and answer just #1 first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused by how the prior assignments differ. I should reduce confusion by distinguishing the functions of each tool, but I must not provide a filled pathway example or any partial solution content. To remain Socratic and bite-sized, I will define each prior assignment by the question it answers, then ask the student to test that framework on one pathway. This moves them toward constructing their own SWOT entries.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe governance or infrastructure? i’m honestly more confused now lol, can u just show a full sample pathway SWOT so i can copy the format.
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to give you a sample to copy. My role is to help you think, not do it for you.

Let’s force clarity: choose one, governance or infrastructure.
Then fill this skeleton yourself:
- Strengths: what internal climate-action assets already exist?
- Weaknesses: what internal gaps slow action?
- Opportunities: what outside openings could the city use?
- Threats: what outside pressures could undermine progress?

Which one are you choosing, and what is one concrete thing your city already has in place there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full sample they could copy, which would violate the rule against providing solutions or writing for them. I should refuse that specific request, keep boundaries clear, and offer minimal scaffolding that helps them generate their own structure. Using Socratic guidance, I can give a blank template and force a choice between the pathways they named, then ask targeted questions that move toward solution without supplying content.

exchanges.student: ugh ok infrastructure i guess, but i still don’t know what counts as “internal” vs “external” fr. can u just tell me that part clearly?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Keep it this simple:

Internal = things the city already has, controls, or is responsible for.
External = things outside the city’s direct control that affect what it can do.

So:
- Strengths/Weaknesses = internal
- Opportunities/Threats = external

Quick test:
- Existing transit system? internal.
- City budget gap? internal.
- New national funding program? external.
- State politics or extreme weather trends? external.

Now test yourself: name one infrastructure fact about your city, and tell me whether you think it’s internal or external.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will address the student's conceptual confusion directly because they are frustrated, but I will still avoid giving pathway content or a solution. I will use a concise distinction with decision rules and then ask the student to classify one of their own city facts, which keeps the exchange Socratic and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: ok maybe “aging drainage system” is internal? but then flooding is external? also tbh i kinda want a bigger explanation bc i’m scared i’m oversimplifying it.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—those are heading in the right direction.

Aging drainage system = internal because it’s part of the city’s infrastructure condition/capacity.
Flooding = usually external as a hazard/stressor affecting the city.

Don’t overcomplicate it: ask, “Is this a condition of the city system, or a pressure acting on it?”
- city system condition/capacity = internal
- outside pressure/opening = external

They connect, but they’re not the same. Weak drainage can make the external threat of flooding worse.

Now keep moving: for infrastructure in your city, what is one existing asset and one gap?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's classification without fully resolving the SWOT entries for them. Since they asked for a bigger explanation and are anxious, I can give a slightly fuller conceptual distinction plus a decision rule and caution about interaction between categories, while still keeping it bite-sized and ending with a forward-moving question. This remains within the tutor role and avoids writing the assignment.

exchanges.student: maybe asset = some flood control projects, gap = old pipes in a lot of neighborhoods. that didn’t totally fix it tho, can u explain how to connect this back to the spider diagram + actor map too?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now connect each tool to a different job:

- Spider Diagram: tells you whether infrastructure is a priority problem or relative strength.
- Actor Map: tells you who can fix the old pipes or expand flood control, and who might resist.
- Stressors: tell you why infrastructure matters now—what hazards are pressuring the system.

So ask yourself:
1. Did infrastructure score low or high on your Spider Diagram?
2. Which 2 actors matter most for infrastructure?
3. Which stressor makes this pathway urgent?

Answer those 3, and your SWOT categories will start to sort themselves.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still confused but now has two concrete ideas. I should help them integrate prior assignments into the SWOT process without supplying content. To follow the anti-redundancy rule, I will explicitly connect each previous assignment to a different decision the student makes in SWOT, then ask them to apply it to the pathway they already chose. This keeps the answer short, directive, and solution-oriented.

exchanges.student: i don’t really remember my spider score rn, and i’m still not seeing how all 3 become SWOT without it turning into the same point repeated 4 times. can u lay out the conversion more directly?
exchanges.tutor:
More directly:

Use the same evidence for different purposes, not as repeats.
- Spider Diagram → tells you where the pathway stands overall: likely strength or weakness.
- Actor Map → tells you implementation capacity/conflict: can become strength, weakness, opportunity, or threat depending on the actor’s role.
- Stressors → usually feed threats, and sometimes justify opportunities.

For infrastructure:
- city has flood-control projects → likely Strength
- old pipes across neighborhoods → likely Weakness
- outside funding/partnership window → Opportunity
- worsening flood risk/extreme rain → Threat

Notice: each point is doing a different job.

Now you try: take one actor from your map. Does that actor belong more naturally under strength/weakness or opportunity/threat, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on integrating the three prior tools into SWOT and asks for a more direct mapping. I should be slightly less Socratic because of frustration, but still avoid giving content or a sample solution. I will provide a compact 'conversion' rule set and emphasize non-duplication by assigning each source a different role. Then I will ask the student to apply it to one of their own facts.

exchanges.student: maybe the city public works dept is a strength if it’s active, or a weakness if it’s underfunded? idk, i’m still kinda stuck and wanna see the whole thing together tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s the right logic. Same actor, different SWOT category depending on the evidence.

We’ve circled this idea enough. Now you need to turn it into your own draft instead of asking for another explanation.

Write 4 bullets for infrastructure only:
- Strength: one internal asset
- Weakness: one internal gap
- Opportunity: one external opening
- Threat: one external pressure

Under each bullet, add: “How do I know?” using your spider diagram, actor map, or stressors.

Post that 4-bullet attempt here. I’ll critique it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking again for the whole thing together after several exchanges on the same concept. Per the rules, I should address the redundancy by asking them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt. I will not provide a full sample or partial solution. I will confirm their reasoning, then give a compact fill-in structure they must complete themselves, which moves directly toward the assignment without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: ok fine, here’s my rough try: strength = some flood control projects; weakness = aging pipes; opportunity = maybe federal/state resilience money; threat = heavier rain/flooding. idk if that’s too generic tho.
exchanges.tutor:
This is a workable start, but yes—right now it’s too generic for the assignment.

Pressure-test each bullet:
- Which flood control projects, specifically?
- How widespread or serious are the aging pipes?
- What exact funding source or program are you pointing to?
- What evidence shows heavier rain/flooding is a real threat in your city?

Now pick just one bullet—your strongest one—and make it concrete with one policy, project, actor, or data point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced a draft, so the best tutoring move is to evaluate its specificity and push them to strengthen it using evidence from prior assignments. I will not rewrite or expand their bullets for them. I will be critical but constructive, ask targeted questions that make each item more concrete, and keep the response short.
